thread_tweets.full_text: AMD and AAPL both, in different ways, have announced a focus on flash as a way to reduce the AI memory tax. We wrote about this in our State of the Themes last week. https://t.co/IA9w0iX9vO

thread_tweets.text:
AI workloads are creating new memory objects that are too large for HBM but too valuable for cold storage. It’s simply to expensive to use DRAM for everything if it’s possible to use NAND for some of it. We’ve gotten three announcements focused on flash from three major players in the past couple weeks:

Nvidia is using flash to store reusable KV cache via CMX. This targets long-context, multi-turn and agentic inference by extending GPU memory with a shared KV-cache tier, allowing flash to become context memory for long-running agents.

AMD is using flash to store some system memory until it is predicted to be hot enough to go to DRAM. They’re buying MEXT as a software layer to accomplish this. 

Apple is using flash to store some model weights until needed. Apple stores inactive experts in flash on the device side (AFM 3 Core Advanced, published a week ago).

If you can get the system to know what data is likely to be needed next, flash goes from simple storage to cheaper memory.

thread_tweets.text:
Whether this is yet another jevons paradox situation that simply means the DRAM shortage remains as severe but we use it even more effectively, or something that lays the groundwork for DRAM margins to get hit as lower margin (and easier-to-produce, especially by China) flash becomes more capable is in the eye of the beholder.

But I certainly would not be shorting flash right now.

AMD is using flash to store some system memory until it is predicted to be hot enough to go to DRAM. They’re buying MEXT as a software layer to accomplish this. 

Apple is using flash to store some model weights until needed. Apple stores inactive experts in flash on the device side (AFM 3 Core Advanced, published a week ago).

If you can get the system to know what data is likely to be needed next, flash goes from simple storage to cheaper memory.

Whether this is yet another jevons paradox situation that simply means the DRAM shortage remains as severe but we use it even more effectively, or something that lays the groundwork for DRAM margins to get hit as lower margin (and easier-to-produce, especially by China) flash becomes more capable is in the eye of the beholder.

thread_tweets.full_text: @choffstein @JeffiniteVC The whole point of my tweet is bucket shop clients did 50x with their whole stack and so do retail perp traders.

thread_tweets.text:
So this post was meant to illustrate one concept and was not meant to be an exhaustive compendium of all concepts associated with perp markets and   exchanges. 

Specifically SPX perps have 50x leverage available. That allows someone to use their entire net worth to make a leveraged bet on SPX that has a pretty high chance of being auto-liquidated. Just as in a bucket shop.

 I understand and of course can be wrong that many participants risk their entire AUM in a 50/1 leveraged bet. Of course if the same dude opened a futures brokerage account they could buy ES futures at 20/1 with their whole stack.  That's pretty dumb to do as well but nowhere near as dumb in terms of likelihood of being liquidated. 

Somehow and I am still learning but it seems those who wanted to bet their whole stack are much more common on hyperliquid and binance than they ever have been in a tradfi futures brokerage account. Perhaps it's the 50/1 leverage provided or perhaps it's the "cool kids" destination or something else.  But I suspect it's because a futures brokerage has full recourse and standing when a margin called is not met and the brokerage takes a loss on liqudidation.  The perp trader can walk away with no recourse.  This is similar to an old school bucket shop.  No recourse when liquidated.  Anyway it seems that perp exchanges have been successful in getting speculators to bet their full stack at high leverage.  Just like a bucket shop.

This is the narrow point of the tweet. 

20/1 or 50/1 is pretty irrelevant for some other trading strategies.  For intstance when I want to lever up my long equity portfolio which has say $100 in AUM.  Let's say I want to control $120 notional and be leveraged.  I can buy $20 of futures and use just $1 of collateral from my AUM.  Or I could buy $20 of perps and only pledge $0.40 of my AUM.  Notice I use 20x leverage on futures and 50x leverage on perps BUT i am only 1.2x leveraged on my AUM.  

Frankly they are roughly equivalent for me as user.  I can meet margin calls with my AUM or frankly overcollateralize my perp brokerage    It's pretty darn safe.  I get my leverage.  Most of my stack is safe and my leverage is 1.2x.  Perps used in this way are as you said like literally all futures (though more leveraged).  When used like this I'm cool with both

Again the point of the tweet is betting all your stack on a 50x or 20x leveraged thing is degenerate gambling with little difference than a bucket shop.  Perp exchanges encourage this behavior.  Futures brokerages don't

tweets.text:
The SEC might have just opened one of the biggest doors for tokenized stocks.

It proposed removing Rule 611, which basically says stock venues need to respect the best visible price across the market.

Right now, US stocks trade through venues like NYSE and Nasdaq.

But if stocks get tokenized, people will want to trade them like crypto across different DeFi venues.

This is where @Uniswap (and AMMs in general) come in.

Uniswap is the clearest example of pool-based pricing because it does not use a traditional order book like NYSE or Nasdaq.

But that is also the problem.

Because AMMs do not naturally follow Rule 611, they price trades through pool liquidity, not by checking every stock exchange before every swap.

And that’s why removing Rule 611 matters. It would remove one of the biggest structural blockers for DeFi-native stock markets.

If this passes, it’s bullish for DeFi infra tokens, RWA protocols, and DEX platforms.

And is negotiating large-scale purchase orders for CW Lasers to ensure its production capacity is not constrained by $NVDA (Trendforce)

- $SIVE (AMD went to GFS for CPO, Sivers reference laser level)
- $AAOI (Rosenblatt analyst checks)

I predicted this last year and said hyperscalers should go more upstream to secure capacity... at laser levels, epiwafer levels, or even inp substrate levels. 

To not get bottlenecked by Nvidia.

tweets.text:
I think it’s time to revisit the accredited investor laws in the US.

Companies are staying private longer, where only accredited investors (aka rich people!) can invest. Retail investors can only come in after IPO, when much of the upside has already been captured.

These rules were created with the best of intentions, to protect regular people from scams - a noble idea. Unfortunately, in practice they've often made it illegal to get richer, unless you're already rich. A regressive tax!

We have to judge policies based on their outcomes, not on their intentions.

These are two possible routes I see:

1) Replace the rule with something merit-based, like a financial literacy test. Pass it and you're accredited. Having a qualification based on competency rather than your bank balance or income seems far more fair.

2) Remove the rule entirely. Let consenting adults assess their own risk. Disclosure requirements stay and fraud enforcement stays to punish bad actors.

Many of my friends were shorting TSLA at some point, all got rekt...it was kind of like a rite of passage in the social circle that came out of our university investor's club

One guy got super obsessed, kept adding to his short while citing some equally obsessed analysts who were sending drones to count cars on parking lots and developing bearish theses based on how much is made, turned over, yadayada

(Coincidentally he also wrote his master's thesis on cryptocurrencies in 2014, but concluded they are a sham and never touched them...imagine if he'd bought BTC instead of shorting TSLA)

It turns out TSLA is not a car company, market doesn't care about how many cars it sells or about its financials at all (it still trades at like 350 P/E)

I reckon SpaceX will have the same energy squared

We are going to build a moonbase and then colonize Mars...who cares about financials

In the wake of the SpaceX IPO, with space being the final frontier, I think it is time for science to rethink their fundamental theories.

This is the best view we have of atoms. There is a seemingly natural balance here.

Those nuclei with two lights are plus charged. Those electrons with no light are negatively charged and those with one are neutral.

If instead of looking at this as mass and energy, let’s just look at it as energy.

As my friend Cree Edwards theorized, “we are all energy.”

I think we will need to start looking at space in a new light for space travel to take another anthropological leap, but in the meantime, we have SpaceX.

Go Elon!

tweets.text:
haven’t been back to Michigan since my high school graduation, now headed back for @reindsummit and having all the feels! 

i moved to America (from the Netherlands) as a kid in the mid 90s. i’ve been here for most of my life, 30 years come July, and am grateful every day to be living the American dream 🇺🇸

America is the greatest country on earth, full stop. there is a pervasive negativity about America and its future that i see on the timeline and it makes me sad. we cannot tolerate this version of our shared lore to spread. we must stamp it out with unbridled optimism and fearless action. 

for all its faults, America is the only place on earth with unlimited social and economic mobility for people who want to work hard and tackle ambitious problems, where people can raise billions of dollars to build insane things like fusion reactors and starships, and where failure is not only accepted but encouraged as part of the process of progress. 

we live in the most incredible time in human history, in an age of unprecedented abundance, where miracles happen every day. we must shake off this secular spiritual stagnation and embrace America’s awesomeness. yes we have problems *and* yes we can solve them. 

excited to hang with a bunch of badass humans this week and celebrate the potential to rebuild of America’s industrial base, from factory floor to frontier lab. 

long America forever 🫶

openai recently invested a lot of capital into deploying gpt in every business, home in the world. this is very strategic (and smart) for a non- obvious reason:

data and intents - the greater surface area GPT covers, the more data they collect around customer intentions, workflow traces, product intuition etc

that is a goldmine for model training but the nuttiest part is this used to be proprietary data

now both model lab and businesses are teaming up to create wins for each other

tweets.text:
Every generation of software made humans more productive. Excel made accountants more productive. CRM made salespeople more productive. ERP made operations more productive.

But all of it automated process, never judgment. The tacit knowledge of your best people (the pattern recognition, the contextual decisions, the expertise that made a twenty-year veteran irreplaceable) software could systematize around it but never encode it.

Token capital is the first system that crosses that line. It doesn't just enhance human judgment. It learns it, encodes it, and compounds it back into the organization with every decision made.

Token capital compounds human capital.

The sovereignty test is does your company's knowledge live inside the model (in which case the model provider owns it, not you) or outside it, in private evals, knowledge bases, organizational memory you control. The firms that win own everything below the model layer because it makes every future token cheaper and smarter than the last.

Firms won't own their own models. The capex is prohibitive and platforms like Microsoft will commoditize model access entirely. The real IP lives in the layer underneath.
